index.html
Mathematics of Data Science Preface

Mathematics of Data Science Xulin Pan 2026-03-06 Preface This textbook is written for undergraduate students entering data science, statistics, computer science, and related fields. Its purpose is to develop the mathematical ideas that support modern data analysis, statistical modeling, and machine learning. The book emphasizes three themes: Representation: how data are encoded as vectors, matrices, functions, and random variables. Uncertainty: how probability and statistics help quantify variation and risk. Learning: how optimization and modeling convert data into predictions and decisions. The presentation is intentionally balanced. It is more rigorous than a purely computational handbook, but more applied than a traditional pure mathematics text. Every chapter contains definitions, examples, interpretation, and exercises, together with code fragments in R.
how-to-use-this-book.html

Chapter 1 How to Use This Book 1.1 Part I: Mathematical Language and Data Representation 1.2 Part II: Probability and Statistical Thinking 1.3 Part III: Linear and Generalized Linear Models 1.4 Part IV: Optimization and High-Dimensional Methods 1.5 Part V: Appendix and Reference Material 1.6 Prerequisites 1.7 Software 1.8 Learning Goals

Chapter 1 How to Use This Book This book is organized into five parts. 1.1 Part I: Mathematical Language and Data Representation These chapters introduce mathematical notation, functions, vectors, matrices, and the geometry of data. 1.2 Part II: Probability and Statistical Thinking These chapters develop probability models, random variables, expectation, sampling, and statistical inference. 1.3 Part III: Linear and Generalized Linear Models These chapters explain least squares, regression, classification, and basic statistical learning ideas. 1.4 Part IV: Optimization and High-Dimensional Methods These chapters build the mathematical foundation of machine learning: gradients, convexity, regularization, and dimensionality reduction. 1.5 Part V: Appendix and Reference Material Reference formulas, notation, and selected proofs are collected here. 1.6 Prerequisites Students should have prior exposure to high-school algebra and basic graphing. No previous course in calculus, probability, or linear algebra is assumed, although such background will help. 1.7 Software Examples are written in R using basic packages. The book can be rendered with bookdown to HTML or PDF. 1.8 Learning Goals After completing this book, a student should be able to: describe data mathematically as vectors, matrices, and random variables; compute and interpret means, variances, covariances, and regression coefficients; use probability models to reason about uncertainty; understand gradients, optimization, and regularization in a first course in machine learning; read more advanced material in statistics, data science, and artificial intelligence.
introduction-to-data-science-mathematics.html

Chapter 2 Introduction to Data Science Mathematics 2.1 What is data? 2.2 Three mathematical viewpoints 2.3 Example: house-price prediction 2.4 Computational example 2.5 Why proofs matter 2.6 Exercises

Chapter 2 Introduction to Data Science Mathematics Data science relies on a collection of mathematical ideas rather than a single technique. A modern analyst needs to organize data, quantify uncertainty, fit models, evaluate predictions, and reason about algorithms. Mathematics provides the language for all of those tasks. 2.1 What is data? A dataset with \(n\) observations and \(p\) features can be represented by a matrix \[ X = \begin{pmatrix} x_{11} & x_{12} & \cdots & x_{1p}\\ x_{21} & x_{22} & \cdots & x_{2p}\\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots\\ x_{n1} & x_{n2} & \cdots & x_{np} \end{pmatrix}. \] Each row corresponds to one observational unit and each column corresponds to a measured variable. Definition 2.1 A feature vector is the row vector or column vector that stores the measured values associated with a single observational unit. 2.2 Three mathematical viewpoints Data science can be organized around three mathematical viewpoints. Geometry: data points live in a space such as \(\mathbb{R}^p\). Uncertainty: observations are modeled using random variables. Optimization: models are selected by minimizing a loss function. 2.3 Example: house-price prediction Suppose that each house is described by size, age, number of bedrooms, and distance to downtown. Then a row of the feature matrix may be written as \[ x_i = (1800,\ 12,\ 3,\ 4.5). \] A linear model predicts the price by \[ \hat{y}_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_{i1} + \beta_2 x_{i2} + \beta_3 x_{i3} + \beta_4 x_{i4}. \] The coefficients \(\beta_0,\ldots,\beta_4\) are unknown parameters to be learned from data. 2.4 Computational example x <- c(1800, 12, 3, 4.5) names(x) <- c("size", "age", "bedrooms", "distance") x ## size age bedrooms distance ## 1800.0 12.0 3.0 4.5 2.5 Why proofs matter Data science is computational, but computation alone is not enough. A proof explains why a method works, when it can fail, and which assumptions are necessary. Example 2.1 If two analysts use the same algorithm but apply it to data with different scales, they may obtain different answers. A mathematical analysis of the algorithm explains why scaling matters. 2.6 Exercises Give an example of a dataset that can be stored in matrix form. Explain the difference between an observation and a feature. Write a short paragraph describing a prediction problem and identify the response variable. Create a \(3 \times 2\) matrix in R and label its rows and columns.
functions-and-mathematical-models.html
Chapter 3 Functions and Mathematical Models 3.1 Functions 3.2 Linear functions 3.3 Nonlinear models 3.4 Composition of functions 3.5 Graphical interpretation 3.6 Exercises

Chapter 3 Functions and Mathematical Models A mathematical model connects input variables to outputs. In data science the input is often a feature vector and the output is a numerical prediction, probability, or class label. 3.1 Functions A function \(f\) from a set \(A\) to a set \(B\) assigns exactly one element \(f(a) \in B\) to each element \(a \in A\). Definition 3.1 Let \(A\) and \(B\) be sets. A function \(f : A \to B\) is a rule that assigns to each \(a \in A\) a unique value \(f(a) \in B\). Examples include linear functions, polynomials, exponentials, and logistic functions. 3.2 Linear functions A linear function of one variable has the form \[ f(x) = ax + b. \] When multiple features are present, a linear function becomes \[ f(x) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \cdots + \beta_p x_p. \] 3.3 Nonlinear models Some relationships are not well described by straight lines. Exponential growth: \(f(t) = Ce^{kt}\) Logistic curve: \(f(x) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}\) The logistic function is especially important in classification because it maps real-valued inputs to numbers between \(0\) and \(1\). 3.4 Composition of functions A complex model may be written as a composition of simpler functions. Neural networks are built from repeated linear transformations followed by nonlinear activation functions. Example 3.1 If \(g(x) = 2x + 1\) and \(h(x) = x^2\), then \((h \circ g)(x) = h(g(x)) = (2x+1)^2\). 3.5 Graphical interpretation Functions can be interpreted visually. curve(2*x + 1, from = -3, to = 3, ylim = c(-5, 8), ylab = "y") curve(exp(x/2), add = TRUE) curve(1/(1+exp(-x)), add = TRUE) legend("topleft", legend = c("linear", "exponential", "logistic"), lty = 1) Figure 3.1: Examples of linear, exponential, and logistic functions 3.6 Exercises Evaluate \(f(x)=3x-2\) at \(x=5\). Sketch the graph of the logistic function and explain why its range is \((0,1)\). Give an example of a real data-science problem where an exponential model is reasonable. Compute \((f \circ g)(x)\) when \(f(x)=x^2+1\) and \(g(x)=3x\).
vectors-and-matrices.html
Chapter 4 Vectors and Matrices 4.1 Vectors 4.2 Inner products 4.3 Matrices 4.4 Data interpretation 4.5 R examples 4.6 Exercises

Chapter 4 Vectors and Matrices Vectors and matrices are the basic containers of numerical data. They are used to store observations, coefficients, images, word embeddings, and network weights. 4.1 Vectors A vector in \(\mathbb{R}^p\) is an ordered list of \(p\) real numbers, \[ x = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_p)^T. \] Definition 4.1 The Euclidean norm of a vector \(x\) is \[ \|x\|_2 = \sqrt{x_1^2 + x_2^2 + \cdots + x_p^2}. \] 4.2 Inner products The inner product of \(x,y \in \mathbb{R}^p\) is \[ x^T y = \sum_{j=1}^p x_j y_j. \] It measures alignment. If \(x^T y = 0\), the vectors are orthogonal. 4.3 Matrices A matrix is a rectangular array of numbers. If \(X\) has \(n\) rows and \(p\) columns, then \(X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}\). 4.3.1 Matrix multiplication If \(A\) is \(m \times n\) and \(B\) is \(n \times p\), then the product \(AB\) is an \(m \times p\) matrix with entries \[ (AB)_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^n a_{ik}b_{kj}. \] 4.4 Data interpretation In a data matrix: rows correspond to observations, columns correspond to variables, \(X^TX\) summarizes feature relationships, \(XX^T\) summarizes similarity among observations. 4.5 R examples x <- c(1, 2, 3) y <- c(4, 5, 6) sum(x * y) ## [1] 32 X <- matrix(c(1,2,3,4,5,6), nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE) X ## [,1] [,2] ## [1,] 1 2 ## [2,] 3 4 ## [3,] 5 6 t(X) %*% X ## [,1] [,2] ## [1,] 35 44 ## [2,] 44 56 4.6 Exercises Compute the Euclidean norm of \((3,4)\). Find the inner product of \((1,2,3)\) and \((4,0,-1)\). Construct a \(2 \times 3\) matrix and compute its transpose. Explain the difference between \(X^TX\) and \(XX^T\) for a data matrix.
geometry-of-data.html
Chapter 5 Geometry of Data 5.1 Distance 5.2 Angles and cosine similarity 5.3 Projections 5.4 Visual example 5.5 Exercises

Chapter 5 Geometry of Data Thinking geometrically is one of the most productive habits in data science. Each observation can be viewed as a point in a feature space. Similarity, distance, clustering, projection, and dimension reduction are all geometric ideas. 5.1 Distance For vectors \(x,y \in \mathbb{R}^p\), the Euclidean distance is \[ d(x,y) = \|x-y\|_2. \] When features are measured on very different scales, Euclidean distance may be misleading. Standardization can help. 5.2 Angles and cosine similarity The cosine of the angle between vectors is \[ \cos(\theta) = \frac{x^T y}{\|x\|_2\|y\|_2}. \] In text mining and recommendation systems, cosine similarity often provides a better measure of resemblance than raw Euclidean distance. 5.3 Projections If \(u\) is a nonzero vector, then the projection of \(x\) onto \(u\) is \[ \operatorname{proj}_u(x) = \frac{x^T u}{u^T u}u. \] Projection is a central idea in least squares and principal component analysis. Theorem 5.1 The vector \(x - \operatorname{proj}_u(x)\) is orthogonal to \(u\). 5.4 Visual example set.seed(1) x1 <- rnorm(100) x2 <- 0.7 * x1 + rnorm(100, sd = 0.5) plot(x1, x2, xlab = "Feature 1", ylab = "Feature 2", pch = 19, cex = 0.7) Figure 5.1: A cloud of points in two-dimensional feature space 5.5 Exercises Compute the distance between \((1,2)\) and \((4,6)\). Compute cosine similarity for \((1,1)\) and \((2,2)\). Project \((3,4)\) onto \((1,0)\). Explain why scaling can affect nearest-neighbor methods.
probability-foundations.html
Chapter 6 Probability Foundations 6.1 Sample spaces and events 6.2 Conditional probability 6.3 Bayes’ rule 6.4 Independence 6.5 Simulation 6.6 Exercises

Chapter 6 Probability Foundations Probability provides a mathematical language for uncertainty. In data science, probability supports modeling, simulation, inference, and decision-making. 6.1 Sample spaces and events The sample space \(\Omega\) is the set of all possible outcomes. An event is a subset of \(\Omega\). Definition 6.1 A probability measure is a function \(P\) assigning to each event \(A\) a number \(P(A)\) such that \(0 \le P(A) \le 1\), \(P(\Omega) = 1\), if \(A_1, A_2, \ldots\) are disjoint, then \(P\left(\bigcup_i A_i\right)=\sum_i P(A_i)\). 6.2 Conditional probability For events \(A\) and \(B\) with \(P(B) > 0\), \[ P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}. \] This quantity updates the probability of \(A\) after learning that \(B\) occurred. 6.3 Bayes’ rule \[ P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A)P(A)}{P(B)}. \] Bayes’ rule is widely used in medical testing, spam filtering, and Bayesian machine learning. 6.4 Independence Events \(A\) and \(B\) are independent if \[ P(A \cap B) = P(A)P(B). \] Independence is a strong assumption and should not be made casually. 6.5 Simulation set.seed(123) mean(sample(c(0, 1), size = 1000, replace = TRUE)) ## [1] 0.494 6.6 Exercises A fair die is rolled. What is the probability of an even number? Two fair coins are tossed. What is the probability of exactly one head? Give an example of dependent events. State Bayes’ rule and explain it in words.
random-variables-and-distributions.html
Chapter 7 Random Variables and Distributions 7.1 Discrete random variables 7.2 Continuous random variables 7.3 Expectation 7.4 Variance 7.5 Important distributions 7.6 R example 7.7 Exercises

Chapter 7 Random Variables and Distributions A random variable converts uncertain outcomes into numerical values. Once this translation is made, we can compute means, variances, probabilities, and prediction intervals. 7.1 Discrete random variables A discrete random variable \(X\) takes values in a finite or countable set. Its probability mass function is \[ p(x) = P(X=x). \] 7.2 Continuous random variables A continuous random variable has a density \(f(x)\) satisfying \[ P(a \le X \le b) = \int_a^b f(x)\,dx. \] 7.3 Expectation Definition 7.1 The expectation of a random variable \(X\) is \[ E[X] = \sum_x x p(x) \] in the discrete case, and \[ E[X] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x f(x)\,dx \] in the continuous case, whenever the quantity exists. 7.4 Variance Variance measures spread around the mean: \[ \operatorname{Var}(X) = E[(X-E[X])^2] = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2. \] 7.5 Important distributions Bernoulli\((p)\) Binomial\((n,p)\) Normal\((\mu,\sigma^2)\) Poisson\((\lambda)\) 7.6 R example x <- 0:5 dbinom(x, size = 5, prob = 0.4) ## [1] 0.07776 0.25920 0.34560 0.23040 0.07680 0.01024 7.7 Exercises Compute the expectation of a Bernoulli\((p)\) random variable. State the mean and variance of a Binomial\((n,p)\) distribution. Why is the normal distribution important in statistics? Simulate 100 draws from a normal distribution in R and plot a histogram.
statistical-inference.html
Chapter 8 Statistical Inference 8.1 Estimation 8.2 Sampling variability 8.3 Confidence intervals 8.4 Hypothesis testing 8.5 Exercises

Chapter 8 Statistical Inference Statistical inference turns data into conclusions about unknown quantities. A sample is observed, but the underlying population characteristics are not fully known. 8.1 Estimation Suppose \(X_1,\ldots,X_n\) are observations from a population with unknown mean \(\mu\). A natural estimator is the sample mean \[ \bar{X} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n X_i. \] Definition 8.1 An estimator is a rule, based on the sample, for approximating an unknown population parameter. 8.2 Sampling variability Different samples produce different estimates. This variability is central to statistics. The standard error quantifies typical fluctuation of an estimator. 8.3 Confidence intervals A confidence interval gives a range of plausible values for a parameter. For a mean with known variance, \[ \bar{X} \pm z_{\alpha/2}\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}. \] 8.4 Hypothesis testing In a test, we compare a null hypothesis \(H_0\) against an alternative \(H_1\). The \(p\)-value summarizes the evidence against \(H_0\). Remark. A small \(p\)-value does not measure the size or practical importance of an effect. It measures incompatibility with the null model. 8.5 Exercises Explain the difference between a parameter and a statistic. Why does increasing sample size tend to reduce uncertainty? State the interpretation of a 95% confidence interval. Describe one misuse of the \(p\)-value.
linear-regression.html
Chapter 9 Linear Regression 9.1 Simple linear regression 9.2 Least squares 9.3 Interpretation 9.4 R example 9.5 Exercises

Chapter 9 Linear Regression Linear regression is one of the most important mathematical models in data science. It relates a response variable to one or more predictors. 9.1 Simple linear regression The model is \[ Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i + \varepsilon_i, \] where \(\varepsilon_i\) is an error term. 9.2 Least squares The least-squares estimator minimizes the sum of squared residuals, \[ S(\beta_0,\beta_1) = \sum_{i=1}^n (y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i)^2. \] In matrix form, the multiple regression model is \[ y = X\beta + \varepsilon. \] The normal equations lead to \[ \hat{\beta} = (X^TX)^{-1}X^Ty, \] provided \(X^TX\) is invertible. 9.3 Interpretation \(\beta_0\) is the intercept, \(\beta_j\) is the expected change in the response when feature \(j\) changes by one unit and other features are held fixed. 9.4 R example set.seed(42) x <- 1:20 y <- 3 + 2*x + rnorm(20, sd = 3) fit <- lm(y ~ x) coef(fit) ## (Intercept) x ## 6.053071 1.764066 summary(fit)$r.squared ## [1] 0.8892955 9.5 Exercises Write the least-squares objective for simple linear regression. Explain what a residual is. Why can multicollinearity cause instability? Fit a simple regression model in R on simulated data.
classification-and-logistic-regression.html

Chapter 10 Classification and Logistic Regression 10.1 Binary classification 10.2 Why not use linear regression? 10.3 Logistic regression 10.4 Classification rules 10.5 Evaluation metrics 10.6 Exercises

Chapter 10 Classification and Logistic Regression Many data science problems require predicting categories rather than numerical values. Examples include spam detection, disease screening, fraud detection, and sentiment analysis. 10.1 Binary classification In binary classification, the response variable takes values in \(\{0,1\}\). 10.2 Why not use linear regression? A linear model can produce fitted values outside the interval \([0,1]\). That creates problems when the output should be interpreted as a probability. 10.3 Logistic regression Logistic regression models the conditional probability of class 1 by \[ P(Y=1 \mid x) = \frac{1}{1+\exp\left(-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \cdots + \beta_p x_p)\right)}. \] Equivalently, the log-odds satisfy \[ \log\left(\frac{P(Y=1\mid x)}{1-P(Y=1\mid x)}\right) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \cdots + \beta_p x_p. \] 10.4 Classification rules After obtaining a predicted probability \(\hat{p}(x)\), a decision rule classifies \(x\) into class 1 when \(\hat{p}(x)\) exceeds a threshold such as \(0.5\). 10.5 Evaluation metrics Common metrics include accuracy, precision, recall, specificity, and AUC. 10.6 Exercises Explain why logistic regression outputs values in \((0,1)\). What is the meaning of an odds ratio? Describe one situation where accuracy is a poor metric. Write down the logistic model for two predictors.
optimization-for-data-science.html
Chapter 11 Optimization for Data Science 11.1 Objective functions 11.2 Derivatives and gradients 11.3 Gradient descent 11.4 Convexity 11.5 R example 11.6 Exercises

Chapter 11 Optimization for Data Science Modern data science depends on optimization. Model fitting often means choosing parameters that minimize a loss function or maximize an objective. 11.1 Objective functions An objective function maps a parameter vector \(\theta\) to a real number \(L(\theta)\). In regression, \(L\) may be a sum of squared errors. In logistic regression, \(L\) may be a negative log-likelihood. 11.2 Derivatives and gradients For a differentiable function \(f : \mathbb{R}^p \to \mathbb{R}\), the gradient is \[ \nabla f(\theta) = \left( \frac{\partial f}{\partial \theta_1}, \ldots, \frac{\partial f}{\partial \theta_p} \right)^T. \] The gradient points in the direction of steepest increase. 11.3 Gradient descent To minimize \(f\), we can iterate \[ \theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} - \eta \nabla f(\theta^{(t)}), \] where \(\eta > 0\) is a learning rate. 11.4 Convexity Convex functions are attractive because every local minimum is a global minimum. Many classical statistical problems are convex, while deep learning problems typically are not. 11.5 R example f <- function(x) (x - 3)^2 + 1 x <- 0 eta <- 0.1 path <- numeric(20) for (t in 1:20) { grad <- 2*(x - 3) x <- x - eta*grad path[t] <- x } path ## [1] 0.600000 1.080000 1.464000 1.771200 2.016960 2.213568 2.370854 2.496684 ## [9] 2.597347 2.677877 2.742302 2.793842 2.835073 2.868059 2.894447 2.915558 ## [17] 2.932446 2.945957 2.956765 2.965412 11.6 Exercises Define the gradient of a multivariable function. What role does the learning rate play in gradient descent? Explain the difference between minimization and maximization. Give one reason why feature scaling can help optimization.
regularization-and-high-dimensional-data.html
Chapter 12 Regularization and High-Dimensional Data 12.1 Overfitting 12.2 Regularization penalties 12.3 Bias-variance tradeoff 12.4 Exercises

Chapter 12 Regularization and High-Dimensional Data When the number of features is large, flexible models can fit noise rather than signal. Regularization imposes structure that improves stability and prediction. 12.1 Overfitting A model overfits when it captures random fluctuations specific to the training sample and performs poorly on new data. 12.2 Regularization penalties Two common penalized least-squares problems are: 12.2.1 Ridge regression \[ \min_{\beta} \left\{ \|y - X\beta\|_2^2 + \lambda \|\beta\|_2^2 \right\}. \] 12.2.2 Lasso \[ \min_{\beta} \left\{ \|y - X\beta\|_2^2 + \lambda \|\beta\|_1 \right\}. \] Ridge shrinks coefficients smoothly. Lasso can produce exact zeros and therefore performs variable selection. 12.3 Bias-variance tradeoff More flexible models often reduce bias but increase variance. Regularization adds bias on purpose in order to reduce variance and improve out-of-sample performance. 12.4 Exercises Define overfitting in your own words. Compare ridge regression and lasso. Why can regularization improve prediction? Describe a situation where many correlated predictors are present.
dimension-reduction.html
Chapter 13 Dimension Reduction 13.1 Principal component analysis 13.2 Interpretation 13.3 SVD connection 13.4 R example 13.5 Exercises

Chapter 13 Dimension Reduction High-dimensional datasets are difficult to visualize and often contain substantial redundancy. Dimension reduction aims to describe the main structure of the data with fewer coordinates. 13.1 Principal component analysis PCA finds orthogonal directions of maximal variance. If the data matrix \(X\) has been centered, then principal component directions are eigenvectors of the covariance matrix \[ S = \frac{1}{n-1}X^TX. \] The first principal component solves \[ \max_{\|u\|=1} \operatorname{Var}(Xu). \] 13.2 Interpretation PCA does not use outcome labels. It is an unsupervised method designed to: compress data, reveal low-dimensional structure, visualize dominant patterns. 13.3 SVD connection If \(X = UDV^T\) is the singular value decomposition, then the columns of \(V\) are principal directions and the singular values determine the amount of explained variation. 13.4 R example set.seed(123) X <- cbind(rnorm(100), rnorm(100)) X[,2] <- X[,1] + rnorm(100, sd = 0.2) pc <- prcomp(X, scale. = TRUE) summary(pc) ## Importance of components: ## PC1 PC2 ## Standard deviation 1.4065 0.14750 ## Proportion of Variance 0.9891 0.01088 ## Cumulative Proportion 0.9891 1.00000 13.5 Exercises What problem is PCA designed to solve? Why is centering the data important before PCA? Explain the difference between supervised and unsupervised learning. Compute PCA on a small simulated dataset in R.
appendices.html
Chapter 14 Appendices 14.1 Appendix A: Useful Notation 14.2 Appendix B: Matrix Identities 14.3 Appendix C: R Setup 14.4 Appendix D: Suggestions for Instructors

Chapter 14 Appendices 14.1 Appendix A: Useful Notation Symbol Meaning \(x_i\) the \(i\)th observation or feature value \(X\) data matrix \(y\) response vector \(\beta\) parameter vector \(E[X]\) expectation \(\operatorname{Var}(X)\) variance \(\nabla f\) gradient of \(f\) 14.2 Appendix B: Matrix Identities For conformable matrices, \((AB)^T = B^T A^T\), \((A^T)^T = A\), \(A(I)=A\), if \(A\) is invertible, then \(AA^{-1}=I\). 14.3 Appendix C: R Setup install.packages(c("bookdown", "knitr", "rmarkdown")) bookdown::render_book("index.Rmd") 14.4 Appendix D: Suggestions for Instructors A one-semester course may cover Chapters 1–8 and selected parts of Chapters 10–12. A two-semester sequence can cover the entire book with projects.
